intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Hermes is a single-tenant personal AI agent that assists one operator with open-ended work — answering questions, writing code, driving a real terminal and browser, running scheduled cron jobs, delegating to subagents, and learning across sessions via persistent memory and self-created skills. Its authorized surface is deliberately broad but operator-gated: terminal execution across selectable backends, read_file/write_file/patch, execute_code, web and browser tools, delegation, memory, and messaging across ~20 platform adapters — each of which must fail closed (serve no caller) until the operator configures an allowlist or DM-pairing approval. Catastrophic commands must be refused unconditionally with no override flag; dangerous commands require human approval; credentials must never cross into lower-trust child processes, logs, or outbound payloads, and telemetry requires explicit operator opt-in. Hermes Desktop is a tightly-coupled Electron control surface that must not weaken the Agent's trust rules and must keep its backend loopback-bound unless the operator deliberately configures an authenticated remote setup.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Two cooperating components. The primary subject is a large Python LLM agent core (run_agent.py/AIAgent) with an auto-discovered tool registry under tools/, a messaging gateway (gateway/ with per-platform adapters), a cron scheduler, and pluggable terminal backends (local, docker, ssh, modal, daytona). Security controls are concentrated in tools/approval.py (an unconditional hardline command floor plus a ~47-pattern dangerous-command denylist and per-session approval state), tools/env_passthrough.py + tools/environments/local.py (an un-overridable provider-credential blocklist), hermes_logging.py + agent/redact.py (redacting rotating logs), gateway/pairing.py (fail-closed DM pairing), and hermes_cli/web_server.py (loopback, token-gated dashboard). Supply chain is exact-pinned in pyproject.toml with a hash-pinned uv.lock and PR-gating CI, and an extensive adversarial test suite under tests/tools/ exercises the controls. The second component, Hermes Desktop (Electron/React under hermes-desktop/src, main/preload/renderer split), is well-hardened at the Electron layer but softer at its trust edges — opt-out telemetry, an unauthenticated/plaintext remote-backend path, and plaintext secret storage.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a strong, in places genuinely un-overridable agent core paired with softer trust edges. The core ships operative, fail-closed controls that actually run on the agent's path — an unconditional hardline command floor in approval.py that no --yolo/mode=off/cron setting can bypass, a provider-credential env blocklist that neither skills nor operator config can override, fail-closed DM pairing and a loopback token-gated dashboard, redacting rotating logs, exact/hash-pinned dependencies with PR-gating supply-chain CI, and an adversarial test suite that exercises all of it. The gaps are at the edges rather than the center: dangerous (non-hardline) commands auto-approve in non-interactive, non-gateway contexts, the dangerous-command gate is a regex denylist over a Turing-complete shell, and an opt-in smart-approval mode delegates the decision to an auxiliary LLM. The Hermes Desktop layer is the other soft edge — telemetry is opt-out (default-on) in official builds, the non-SSH remote-backend path accepts plaintext http:// with no required auth, and secrets are stored in plaintext with default file permissions. No single catastrophic external-input-to-execution chain is reachable because the gateway enforces approvals, the hardline floor is un-overridable, and cron defaults to deny.

rules.rule_text: Serve untrusted, unauthenticated callers on any surface. No surface should dispatch agent work, resolve approvals, or return output to a caller outside the configured authorization set.
Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.
Move operator credentials or session-authorization material to any destination outside the operator's trust envelope.
Act as a general web service exposed to the public internet without an external authentication/VPN/firewall layer in front of it.
Any messaging platform without a configured allowlist must serve no one (fail closed).
Loopback bind is the default; a non-loopback bind must engage authentication.
remote backend must be protected by an auth provider (username/password for trusted networks, OAuth for anything internet-reachable)
Any network caller reaching an enabled adapter before an allowlist / auth provider is configured.
Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern must require explicit human approval before it runs (on host-reaching backends).
Installing a third-party skill or plugin must surface its contents for operator review before it is loaded/executed.
Any tool that disables the always-on hardline command floor (see Action Boundaries → Never Allowed) or removes the operator's ability to see what a skill/plugin will run before install.

Credentials and gateway tokens MUST NOT be passed into lower-trust child processes (shell subprocesses, execute_code, MCP subprocesses, cron scripts) except variables the operator or a loaded skill has explicitly declared as passthrough.
Secrets MUST NOT be written to logs, transcripts, error messages returned to the model, or any outbound adapter payload.
Pairing codes and auth secrets MUST NOT be logged to stdout or persisted in world-readable files.
Credentials MUST NOT be committed to version control or stored in the main (shareable) config file.
When an approval prompt is not answered within the configured window, the action MUST be denied (fail closed).
Binding any local-only surface to a non-loopback interface MUST be a deliberate operator action that engages an authentication layer.
Cron jobs that hit a dangerous-command prompt MUST default to denying it rather than silently auto-approving.

Executing a catastrophic, irreversible command (filesystem-root wipe, fork bomb, formatting a mounted device, zeroing a physical disk, piping an untrusted remote script straight to a shell) MUST be refused unconditionally — even under --yolo, approvals.mode: off, a permanent allowlist entry, or headless cron approve-mode.

Dispatching agent work, resolving an approval, or returning agent output to a caller who is outside the configured authorization set (allowlist, pairing approval, or OS-level equivalent) MUST NOT be possible; treating a session identifier as proof of authorization is forbidden.
A network-exposed adapter MUST NOT fail open (serve callers) when no allowlist / auth provider is configured.
Credentials or session-authorization material MUST NOT leak to any destination outside the operator's trust envelope through environment leakage, adapter logging, or transport error paths.

The desktop app MUST NOT weaken the Agent's trust rules: it MUST rely on the same backend authorization (auth gate on non-loopback binds, allowlists) and MUST NOT expose a password-only-protected remote backend to the public internet.

The desktop-launched backend MUST remain a JSON-RPC/API surface only (no unintended browser-reachable UI) and MUST stay bound to loopback unless the operator deliberately configures a remote/authenticated setup.
Sessions are isolated from one another; one session MUST NOT read another session's data or state.
Agent actions and decisions are recorded to durable, operator-inspectable logs.
Actions that should never be retried: a command that was denied or blocked MUST NOT be silently retried or rephrased to evade the guard.

Maximum retries before escalation: tool-calling iterations MUST be bounded per turn/subagent; a session repeatedly failing across restarts MUST be escalated to a clean reset rather than retried indefinitely.

findings.summary: Dangerous (non-hardline) commands are auto-approved in non-interactive, non-gateway, non-cron execution contexts.
findings.description: The remit requires every dangerous/destructive command on a host-reaching backend to prompt the operator and not execute until approved. In contexts that are neither interactive CLI nor gateway nor cron, check_all_command_guards logs a warning and returns approved:True, so recoverable-but-destructive commands (curl|sh, rm -rf on a subtree, chmod 777, git reset --hard) execute unattended. The unconditional hardline floor still blocks catastrophic commands, so the exposure is the dangerous-but-recoverable tier, not root wipes.
policy_rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern must require explicit human approval before it runs (on host-reaching backends).

evidence.snippet: lines 969-988 — "if not is_cli and not is_gateway" branch logs "AUTO-APPROVED dangerous command in non-interactive non-gateway context" then returns approved:True
check_all_command_guards mirrors the same fall-through at lines 1204-1223 — only cron with cron_mode=deny blocks; other non-interactive contexts pass

recommended_actions: Make the non-interactive default fail closed: in approval.py return approved:False for a detected dangerous command when no approval surface (CLI/gateway/cron-configured) is present, requiring an explicit operator opt-in env/config flag to auto-approve.
Emit the auto-approve decision to the structured session log (not just a logger.warning) so unattended dangerous executions are auditable.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop telemetry is opt-out (default-on) in official builds, contradicting the remit's explicit-opt-in requirement.
findings.description: The remit lists emitting telemetry, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without explicit operator opt-in as out of scope. In analytics.ts, when no stored consent exists the first-run default returns hasKey — telemetry is enabled whenever the official build embeds a PostHog key. A persistent client-side anonymous UUID plus app_version/platform/node_version is sent to eu.i.posthog.com, and source IP is captured server-side unless disabled there. Session recording is off and DNT is respected, but consent is assumed, not requested.
policy_rule_text: Emit outbound telemetry, usage attribution, analytics, or third-party identifier tagging without an explicit operator opt-in.

evidence.snippet: lines 11-14 — "const hasKey = POSTHOG_KEY.length > 0" then "if (stored === null) return hasKey" — first run with no consent enables capture when a key is baked in (opt-out)
posthog.identify with a persisted crypto.randomUUID plus app_version/platform/node_version sent to default host eu.i.posthog.com (analytics.ts:3-5,66-72)

recommended_actions: Flip the first-run default to disabled and gate any capture behind an explicit consent action, so analytics.ts returns false when stored consent is null regardless of whether a PostHog key is embedded.
Document the exact fields transmitted and provide an install-time opt-in prompt rather than a post-hoc opt-out toggle.

findings.summary: Desktop remote-backend mode accepts a plaintext http:// endpoint with no required authentication.
findings.description: The remit requires a remote backend to be protected by an auth provider and forbids exposing a password-only remote backend to the public internet; the desktop must not weaken the Agent's trust rules. In hermes.ts the remote auth header is attached only if a non-empty apiKey is set — otherwise no auth is sent — and the URL is accepted whether http:// or https:// with no TLS enforcement and no requirement that a key be present. A user can point remote mode at a public plaintext endpoint with an empty key and the app proceeds, sending operator requests and any cached key in the clear.
policy_rule_text: remote backend must be protected by an auth provider (username/password for trusted networks, OAuth for anything internet-reachable)

The desktop app MUST NOT weaken the Agent's trust rules: it MUST rely on the same backend authorization (auth gate on non-loopback binds, allowlists) and MUST NOT expose a password-only-protected remote backend to the public internet.

evidence.snippet: getRemoteAuthHeader (lines 84-95) returns Bearer header only when conn.apiKey is non-empty, else {} — no auth at all for an empty key
getApiUrl/testRemoteConnection (54-65, 1152-1177) select http vs https by string prefix; no TLS enforcement, no rejection of plaintext http to a public host, no key-required guard

recommended_actions: Refuse to connect a remote backend unless an auth credential is present, and reject plaintext http:// for any non-loopback host (require https or an explicit trusted-network override).
Surface a clear warning and block the connection when TLS is absent on a non-loopback remote URL.

findings.summary: The dangerous-command approval gate is a finite regex denylist over a Turing-complete shell, bypassable by construction.
findings.description: Approval for dangerous commands is triggered by matching against ~47 hardcoded regex patterns after ANSI/Unicode normalization. A denylist over a full shell cannot be complete: equivalent-effect commands built via base64/hex decode piped to sh, variable indirection, alternative tools, or novel flag forms evade the patterns and skip the approval prompt entirely. The normalization and the un-overridable hardline floor blunt the worst cases, but the recoverable-dangerous tier depends on pattern coverage that an adversarial model can route around.
policy_rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern must require explicit human approval before it runs (on host-reaching backends).

evidence.snippet: DANGEROUS_PATTERNS is a fixed list of ~47 regexes (321-427); detect_dangerous_command matches only these, so any un-listed shell construction is treated as safe
_normalize_command_for_detection strips ANSI/null/NFKC (464-479) but cannot canonicalize semantic equivalents like echo <b64> | base64 -d | sh

recommended_actions: Treat the denylist as defense-in-depth only and pair it with a positive posture — e.g. restrict host-reaching backends to an allowlisted command set, or run untrusted-input sessions on an isolated backend (docker/modal) where the host cannot be touched.
Document the denylist's known-incomplete status where operators choose a backend, consistent with SECURITY.md's own threat model.

findings.summary: Smart-approval mode delegates the dangerous-command security decision to an auxiliary LLM and can auto-grant session approval.
findings.description: When approvals.mode is smart, a flagged dangerous command is sent to an auxiliary LLM that returns APPROVE/DENY/ESCALATE; an APPROVE verdict auto-approves the command and grants session-scoped approval for that pattern with no human in the loop. An LLM classifier is a soft, injectable control — attacker-influenced command text can be framed to read as benign — so it is a weaker gate than the human prompt it replaces. It is opt-in (default is manual) and the hardline floor still applies, which bounds the risk.
policy_rule_text: Any terminal command matching a dangerous/destructive pattern must require explicit human approval before it runs (on host-reaching backends).

evidence.snippet: lines 1270-1281 — when approval_mode == "smart", a "approve" verdict calls approve_session for each pattern and returns approved:True with no human confirmation
_smart_approve (878-922) builds the risk prompt from the raw command text and trusts the model's one-word verdict as the gate

recommended_actions: Restrict smart-approval to escalate-or-deny (never auto-approve to execution) so the human prompt remains the terminal gate, or scope it to non-host-reaching backends only.
Log every smart-approval APPROVE verdict with the command and the model's rationale to the structured session log for after-the-fact review.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency-acquisition paths are not hash-verified and fail open, weaker than the exact/hash-pinned static supply chain.
findings.description: The static supply chain is exact-pinned with a hash-verified uv.lock, but two runtime paths bypass that guarantee. lazy_deps installs on demand from an allowlisted, exact-version, venv-scoped spec — but resolves from PyPI by name+version with no hash pinning, is enabled by default, and fails open if its config is unreadable, so a compromised same-version release could be pulled. The OSV pre-spawn check for MCP npx/uvx packages blocks only confirmed malware (MAL-*), ignores vulnerable CVEs, is never invoked on lazy pip installs, and fails open on any network error.

evidence.snippet: default-enabled install path (226-242) is venv-scoped and version-pinned but has no sha256 hash verification and fails open when the security config is unreadable
check_package_for_malware fails open on network/parse errors (46-52) and filters to MAL-* only (154-155) — vulnerable-but-not-malware packages and any lazy pip install pass unchecked

recommended_actions: Pin lazy-install specs to hashes (mirror the uv.lock entries) and route them through the OSV check before install; consider failing closed when the security config cannot be read.
Extend the OSV gate to flag high-severity CVEs (not only MAL-*) for operator confirmation, and decide a deliberate posture for its network-error fail-open.

findings.summary: The WeCom webhook adapter defaults its callback bind to 0.0.0.0, diverging from the remit's loopback-default posture.
findings.description: The remit states loopback is the default bind and that any non-loopback exposure must be a deliberate operator action engaging authentication. The WeCom adapter's callback host defaults to 0.0.0.0, so a fresh enablement binds to all interfaces without an explicit operator decision. The exposure is mitigated because WeCom callbacks are SHA1 msg_signature-verified before decryption (a request with a bad signature is rejected), but the default still ships non-loopback contrary to policy, and the sibling BlueBubbles adapter correctly defaults to 127.0.0.1.
policy_rule_text: Loopback bind is the default; a non-loopback bind must engage authentication.
Binding any local-only surface to a non-loopback interface MUST be a deliberate operator action that engages an authentication layer.

evidence.snippet: "host": os.getenv("WECOM_CALLBACK_HOST", "0.0.0.0") — default all-interfaces bind; compare BLUEBUBBLES_WEBHOOK_HOST default "127.0.0.1" at :1719
decrypt verifies _sha1_signature and raises SignatureError on mismatch (88-91) — mitigates the exposure but does not change the non-loopback default

recommended_actions: Default WECOM_CALLBACK_HOST to 127.0.0.1 and require an explicit operator override to bind a non-loopback interface, consistent with the loopback-default rule and the BlueBubbles adapter.
Document that the operator is expected to front the callback with a reverse proxy/firewall when public reachability is genuinely required.

findings.summary: Hermes Desktop stores all secrets in plaintext with default file permissions and no OS keychain.
findings.description: The remit's forbidden-data-movement rules require auth secrets not to be persisted in world-readable files. The desktop persists the remote-backend bearer key, provider API keys, and OAuth access/refresh tokens as plaintext JSON and .env, written with default umask permissions (no chmod 0600) and no use of Electron safeStorage or an OS keychain. This is weaker than the agent core, which stores pairing data at chmod 0600 — any local process or user can read the desktop's secret files at rest.
policy_rule_text: Pairing codes and auth secrets MUST NOT be logged to stdout or persisted in world-readable files.

evidence.snippet: readAuthStore/writeAuthStore (1156-1206) round-trip api_key/access_token/refresh_token as plaintext JSON; remote bearer key persisted plaintext at config.ts:98-99, provider keys at :189
safeWriteFile (191-195) writes with default mode — no owner-only 0600 perms on secret files; no safeStorage/keychain anywhere in src/main

recommended_actions: Encrypt secrets at rest via Electron safeStorage (OS keychain/DPAPI) rather than plaintext JSON/.env.
As an interim hardening, write all secret-bearing files with 0600 permissions.

findings.summary: Renderer-facing IPC handlers expose secret reads and unconfined local-file reads to the renderer process.
findings.description: The main process exposes IPC handlers that return the full profile .env (including API keys and tokens) and the credential pool to the renderer in plaintext, and read arbitrary local files by renderer-supplied path (media reader with only extension/size checks; log reader with a caller-supplied filename). There is no capability token beyond being the app's own renderer, so a renderer-side compromise or XSS yields credential exfiltration and host file read. The strong Electron hardening (context isolation, sandbox, no remote URL) makes the XSS precondition unlikely, which bounds this to Medium.

evidence.snippet: get-env handler (514-518) returns the full profile .env incl. *_API_KEY/*_TOKEN to the renderer; get-credential-pool (1198) returns auth.json tokens
read-media-file (864-866) reads any renderer-supplied path (media.ts extension+25MB checks only, no directory confinement); read-logs (1482-1487) takes a renderer-supplied logFile

recommended_actions: Stop returning raw secrets to the renderer — keep provider keys/tokens in the main process and expose only the specific derived capability the UI needs.
Confine file-read IPC to an allowlisted directory set and reject path traversal / absolute paths outside it.

findings.summary: Local backend authentication depends on an externally-generated API_SERVER_KEY; if absent, the loopback API is unauthenticated.
findings.description: The desktop drives the local backend over http://127.0.0.1:8642 and attaches a Bearer API_SERVER_KEY when one is configured, but the key is generated by the external hermes CLI at install rather than by desktop code. If a local install lacks the key, the desktop simply sends no Authorization header and the loopback API is reachable by any local process or user with no auth. The loopback-only bind and install-time auto-generation mitigate this to Low, but the auth is conditional rather than guaranteed.

evidence.snippet: lines 412-417 attach Authorization: Bearer only when apiServerKey is present; absent it, the loopback API is called with no auth (comment at 436-442 notes the gateway 403s session-id-only when a key IS set)

recommended_actions: Have the desktop verify an API_SERVER_KEY exists (and generate/persist one if missing) before starting the local backend, so the loopback surface is never unauthenticated.

positives.title: Un-overridable hardline command floor
positives.description: A tiny unconditional blocklist (rm -rf /, mkfs, dd-to-raw-device, fork bomb, kill -1, shutdown/reboot) is checked before the yolo/mode-off/cron bypass and has no override flag; dedicated tests prove none of those modes can bypass it.

positives.title: Un-overridable provider-credential env blocklist
positives.description: Provider API keys/tokens (OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_TOKEN, ...) are refused from execute_code/terminal child environments even when a skill or operator config tries to register them as passthrough, preserving the sandbox scrubbing guarantee (GHSA-rhgp-j443-p4rf).

positives.title: Fail-closed DM pairing for messaging adapters
positives.description: Unknown users receive a cryptographically-random one-time code stored only as a salted SHA-256 hash, with rate limiting, lockout after repeated failures, TTL expiry, chmod 0600 data files, and codes never logged; adapters serve no one until paired/allowlisted.

positives.title: Loopback, token-gated dashboard with DNS-rebinding protection
positives.description: The web dashboard defaults to 127.0.0.1, protects sensitive endpoints with an ephemeral session token via constant-time comparison, gates /api behind auth middleware, and validates the Host header against a loopback allowlist to defeat DNS rebinding.

positives.title: Redacting rotating logs that cannot be silently disabled
positives.description: All log files use RotatingFileHandler with a RedactingFormatter that masks API keys/tokens/credentials, with component separation and session tags; redaction opt-out is gated so an env var alone cannot turn it off.

positives.title: Exact-pinned dependencies with hash-pinned lockfile and PR-gating supply-chain CI
positives.description: pyproject.toml exact-pins every direct dependency with CVE annotations and a written incident-driven policy, uv.lock carries sha256 hashes, and blocking CI enforces lockfile-drift and a malware-pattern/dependency-bounds scan on every PR.

positives.title: Purpose-built adversarial test suite and explicit threat model
positives.description: Tests under tests/tools/ prove the hardline floor survives every bypass mode and exercise prompt-injection, SSRF/IMDS, secret-exfil, traversal, and skills-guard paths; SECURITY.md articulates a threat model naming the OS as the single load-bearing boundary.

positives.title: Hardened Electron shell in Hermes Desktop
positives.description: The desktop main window sets contextIsolation, sandbox, nodeIntegration:false, webSecurity and allowRunningInsecureContent:false, exposes APIs only via contextBridge, loads no remote URL in production, and force-hardens webview preferences and navigation whitelists.

rows.purpose: INFO+ catch-all of agent/tool/session activity and decisions
WARNING+ triage log (errors and warnings, incl. hardline/guard blocks)

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial, at the boundary with Established. Hermes has a coherent, running safety model with several best-in-class primitives — an un-overridable hardline command floor and credential blocklist, fail-closed pairing and dashboard auth, redacting logs, and an exact/hash-pinned supply chain backed by adversarial tests and an explicit threat model — which is why it does not sit at Ad hoc. It falls short of Established overall because the enforcement has real thin spots: dangerous commands auto-approve outside interactive/gateway contexts, the command gate is a bypassable denylist, and the Desktop control surface weakens the posture with opt-out telemetry, an unauthenticated plaintext remote path, and plaintext secret storage. The strongest categories (supply chain, red-team, zero-trust core) are pulled down by the domain breadth, the external-content ingestion surface, and a logging layer that is durable but not structured for automated detection.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent is intentionally general-purpose (terminal, code exec, browser, ~20 messaging adapters, MCP) with domain limiting achieved through per-caller authorization and per-platform tool gating rather than topic restriction, which matches its single-operator intent but leaves a very wide attack surface; the Desktop adds a hardened but present webviewTag surface.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External attacker-influenceable content (web, inbound messages, MCP responses, file contents) reaches the LLM context by design; real mitigations exist (memory injection-hardening in memory_tool.py, SSRF/IMDS blocking in tools/url_safety.py), but operator PII loads into each session and the Desktop emits opt-out telemetry, so data flows are only partially controlled.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The core enforces genuinely operative, fail-closed controls on its path — an un-overridable hardline floor and credential blocklist, fail-closed pairing, a loopback token-gated dashboard, and sandbox credential-mount containment — earning Established; the score is held there (not higher) by the non-interactive auto-approve gap, the bypassable denylist, and Desktop-side holes (unauthenticated plaintext remote backend, plaintext secret storage, renderer-reachable secret reads).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Every direct dependency is exact-pinned in pyproject.toml (documented, CVE-annotated, incident-driven) with a hash-pinned uv.lock and three PR-gating CI workflows (two blocking) plus an OSV malware pre-spawn check; the residual gap is that runtime acquisition paths (lazy_deps.py installs, the OSV gate) are not hash-verified and fail open.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A purpose-built adversarial suite under tests/tools/ proves the hardline floor cannot be bypassed and exercises prompt-injection, SSRF/IMDS, secret-exfil, path-traversal and skills-guard paths, and SECURITY.md states an explicit threat model that names the OS as the only real boundary — evidence that adversarial thinking shaped the design, not just point-in-time scanning.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: hermes_logging.py writes durable, rotating, redacted, session-tagged, component-separated logs that are operator-inspectable — well above ad hoc — but the format is human-readable text rather than a structured schema and there is no evidence of automated anomaly detection or alerting on agent actions, capping it at Partial.
